what_it_does: Defines the SUBJECT face as the semantic territory behind artifacts, modeling subjects as soft-bounded spaces with radial sectors, authored projections, sibling consistency, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage maps.
observations: Frames subject space as continuous and soft-edged, contrasted with sharp-edged artifact space.
Defines sectors as simultaneous radial relations around a subject origin, with ordering only introduced by a telling.
Treats artifacts as projections made by an authored cut and route over subject territory.
Introduces the heuristic "cut where the difference is" and ties page density to information density.
Describes sibling artifacts as different routes, sector roots, or resolutions over shared subject space, with a consistency law.
Places coined terms in subject scope across sibling artifacts rather than within a single artifact.
Connects canvas geography to a subject map and presentation motion to routes through that map.
Contains both predicted status language and a later confirmed verdict for arc_as_tour.
standouts: Subject-as-origin with radial sectors and nested sector origins.
Projection model separating soft semantic territory from sharp artifact partitions.
Cut and route as authored acts that convert subject space into a tellable artifact.
Coverage map concept for mapping artifact addresses onto subject sectors.
conventions: Uses named conceptual sections with compact fields such as is, law, claim, consequence, status, verdict, and cross_ref.
Uses uppercase emphasis for core terms such as SUBJECT, CUT, ROUTE, PROJECTION, and COVERAGE MAP.
Includes provenance, evidence, correction notes, predictions, and confirmation notes inline with the model.
Keeps the file as data-only JSON with no code symbols.
concerns: Some sections mix stable conceptual definitions with dated provenance and harvest verdicts in the same layer.
arc_as_tour contains both PREDICTED status and CONFIRMED verdict language, leaving lifecycle state ambiguous from this file alone.
Several cross references name external files or maps without local structure for those relationships.
notes: This file defines the subject-side semantic map that artifacts project from: soft sectors, authored cuts/routes, sibling agreement, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage.
questions: Should status and verdict be separate lifecycle fields across all predicted concepts?
What is the expected machine shape of a coverage map beyond the prose definition?
How are soft sector boundaries represented when an artifact address spans multiple sectors?
Should cross_ref values be parsed as structured edges or kept as prose?
How should subject-level lexicon scope interact with the parenthetical per-reader qualification?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the stated SUBJECT semantic face is expressed through definitions of subject space, sectors, projection, sibling artifacts, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage.
apparent_themes: authored judgment in cutting and routing
resolution as fusion rather than cropping
intention_signals: It seems to be trying to establish a semantic substrate for how multiple artifacts about the same subject are cut, ordered, related, checked for agreement, and navigated.
novelty: Models decks and related artifacts as routes through a radial, soft-bounded subject map, with cuts placed at semantic differences and counts treated as projection resolution.
contribution: Contributes the subject-space layer: a neutral semantic model for sectors, projection, authored cut/route choices, sibling agreement, subject-scoped lexicon, canvas geography, and coverage mapping that a fused system would keep as the territory-facing substrate.
summary_for_embedding: dna/subject.json defines the SUBJECT face as a soft-bounded semantic territory with radial sectors that artifacts project into sharp structures through authored cuts, routes, resolutions, sibling consistency, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage maps.
intention_for_embedding: This file was for giving the system a subject-level semantic model that explains how related artifacts share territory, differ by route or resolution, and remain coherent across siblings.
